title: Digital Marketing Projects
text: Below are selected projects that showcase my work in strategic marketing, campaign development, and brand management. These examples highlight how I apply leadership, data-informed decision making, and creative direction to drive marketing success.

title: Digital Marketing Projects
text:
Ontario California Airport - ONT
This project involved developing a comprehensive digital marketing strategy for Ontario International Airport (ONT) to address brand awareness, customer engagement, and digital growth opportunities. Working in a consulting-style team environment, the project analyzed ONT’s digital presence, customer journey, competitor landscape, and marketing channels to identify strategies that could improve traveler engagement and strengthen the airport’s competitive positioning.
The presentation included a detailed evaluation of ONT’s website, SEO performance, social media presence, audience targeting, and competitive benchmarking against other airports and travel brands. Strategic recommendations focused on improving digital visibility, enhancing customer communication, increasing engagement across digital channels, and supporting long-term brand growth through data-driven marketing initiatives.

Skills Demonstrated

Digital Marketing Strategy: Developed integrated marketing recommendations to improve awareness, engagement, and customer acquisition.
Brand Positioning: Evaluated ONT’s market perception and identified opportunities to strengthen competitive differentiation.
SEO & Website Analysis: Conducted website and search visibility evaluations to identify opportunities for improved online performance.
Competitive Benchmarking: Compared ONT’s digital presence and customer engagement strategies against industry competitors.
Customer Journey Analysis: Assessed traveler touchpoints and engagement opportunities throughout the customer funnel.
Social Media Strategy: Analyzed social media performance and proposed content and engagement improvements.
Team Collaboration & Client Presentation: Worked collaboratively to research, develop, and present strategic recommendations in a professional consulting format.

title: Digital Marketing Projects
text:
SEPHORA Brand Audit & Positioning
This project focused on conducting a brand audit and positioning analysis for Sephora, examining the company’s market presence, customer engagement strategies, and competitive positioning within the beauty and cosmetics industry. The presentation evaluated Sephora’s branding, digital marketing efforts, social media presence, and customer experience to identify strengths and opportunities for improvement.
The project included competitor benchmarking, target audience analysis, and strategic recommendations designed to strengthen brand differentiation and customer loyalty. Insights were synthesized into actionable recommendations related to brand communication, digital engagement, and overall marketing strategy.

Skills Demonstrated

Brand Analysis: Assessed Sephora’s messaging, visual identity, customer engagement, and overall brand perception.
Competitive Benchmarking: Compared Sephora’s market positioning and digital presence against key competitors in the beauty industry.
Strategic Communication: Developed recommendations to enhance brand storytelling, customer connection, and marketing effectiveness.
Consumer Insights: Analyzed target audience behaviors and engagement patterns to support branding recommendations.
Marketing Strategy: Evaluated digital marketing channels and customer experience strategies to identify growth opportunities.
Presentation & Stakeholder Communication: Presented findings and strategic recommendations through a professional visual presentation.


  
  

SEPHORA PRESENTATION

title: Digital Marketing Projects
section: Cal Poly Pomona BRIC Tour Video Production
text:
Cal Poly Pomona BRIC Tour Video Production
Project Link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Yz-NHwnVpJ0&t=1s
This project involved the production of a promotional and informational video showcasing the Cal Poly Pomona BRIC (Bronco Recreation and Intramural Complex), a 165,000-square-foot fitness and wellness center designed to support student wellness and campus life. The video highlighted key features of the facility, including Bronco Peak rock climbing wall, fitness studios, indoor track, lap pool, cardio equipment, intramural sports spaces, and student amenities.
I contributed to the project through videography, creative direction, and post-production editing. My role included capturing footage throughout the facility, assisting with scene planning and shot composition, and editing the final video to create an engaging and informative viewing experience. The project focused on promoting student engagement, increasing awareness of campus resources, and delivering visually compelling digital content tailored to prospective and current students.
The final video reached over 1,300 views and helped communicate the value of the BRIC as an important part of student life at Cal Poly Pomona.

Skills Demonstrated

Video Editing: Edited footage using pacing, transitions, audio synchronization, and storytelling techniques to create a polished final product.
Videography: Captured dynamic footage of campus facilities with attention to framing, movement, and composition.
Content Production: Collaborated on the filming and production of promotional digital media content.
Brand Communication: Helped communicate Cal Poly Pomona’s student wellness and campus life initiatives through visual storytelling.
Audience Engagement: Produced engaging content designed to inform and connect with current and prospective students.
Team Collaboration: Worked closely with collaborators throughout filming and post-production processes.
Digital Media Strategy: Supported the creation of platform-friendly video content optimized for YouTube and student audiences.

title: Digital Marketing Projects
section: Sino Yoga — Digital Marketing Strategy Project
text:
Sino Yoga — Digital Marketing Strategy Project
Client: Sino Yoga
Role: Digital Marketing Consultant (Team Project)
Platform Focus: YouTube & Social Media Marketing

Project Overview
Collaborated with a student consulting team to develop a digital marketing strategy for Sino Yoga, a yoga and wellness brand founded by Yang Qi, who has over 18 years of yoga expertise. The project focused on helping the brand increase awareness, improve YouTube visibility, and expand monetization opportunities through consistent digital content and audience growth strategies.


Skills Demonstrated

Marketing Strategy: Developed digital marketing recommendations to improve brand awareness, audience growth, and monetization opportunities.
Team Coordination: Collaborated with team members to research, analyze, and present a client-focused marketing strategy.
Performance Tracking: Evaluated YouTube SEO, engagement metrics, and channel visibility to identify growth opportunities and measure success.
Brand Positioning: Helped define Sino Yoga’s positioning within the wellness market through competitor and audience analysis.
SEO & Content Optimization: Analyzed YouTube search performance and recommended optimization strategies to improve discoverability and engagement.
Data-Driven Decision Making: Used analytics and audit findings to support strategic marketing recommendations.
Customer Journey Analysis: Assessed the awareness stage of the customer funnel to improve audience acquisition and retention strategies.
Client Consulting: Presented actionable insights and strategic recommendations tailored to the client’s business goals.


  
  

MSDM PRESENTATION

title: Final Client Package
text:
Client Overview
Sino Yoga is a cross-cultural yoga and wellness brand founded by Yang Qi, integrating Traditional Chinese Medicine with yoga practice through her signature Five-Element Meridian Yoga method. The brand is expanding from China into the U.S. market and uses YouTube (@Sinoyoga) as its primary discovery channel, with the long-term goal of monetizing its audience through tiered, subscription-based yoga memberships. Sino Yoga maintains a multi-platform digital presence across YouTube, WeChat, a newly launched Facebook page, and the Sino Yoga website.

title: Final Client Package
text:
Business Objectives






Note


Sino Yoga’s primary growth objectives shape the analytical scope of this engagement:




Increase brand awareness across markets
Build a cohesive multi-channel platform experience
Grow subscriber base across YouTube and the website
Strengthen brand visibility in U.S. and international markets
Improve viewer retention and returning audience rate
Develop a sustainable monetization pathway

title: Final Client Package
text:
Data Source
Big Query data used linked from Google Analytics of the Sino Yoga Website. Also exported old GA4 data before linked to Big Query could occur since Big Query only shows events from when it was linked to GA4 only limited data available.
Data Mart Purpose
This analytics mart supports Sino Yoga’s digital marketing dashboard by standardizing website and campaign KPIs for reporting in Looker Studio. The mart is designed to reduce repeated GA4 event-level queries and create consistent KPI definitions across the team.
Business Questions / KPIs Supported
This mart supports questions such as:

How much website traffic is Sino Yoga receiving?
Which channels drive the most sessions?
Are users engaging with the website?
Which landing pages perform best?
Which devices and locations represent the strongest audience segments?

Big Query
View 1: mart_ga4_session_summary_v1

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW `sino-yoga-494202.analytics_527584579.mart_ga4_session_summary_v1` AS

WITH base_events AS (
  SELECT
    PARSE_DATE('%Y%m%d', event_date) AS event_date,
    user_pseudo_id,

    (
      SELECT value.int_value
      FROM UNNEST(event_params)
      WHERE key = 'ga_session_id'
    ) AS ga_session_id,

    event_name,

    (
      SELECT value.string_value
      FROM UNNEST(event_params)
      WHERE key = 'page_location'
    ) AS page_location,

    (
      SELECT value.int_value
      FROM UNNEST(event_params)
      WHERE key = 'engagement_time_msec'
    ) AS engagement_time_msec,

    traffic_source.source AS source,
    traffic_source.medium AS medium,
    traffic_source.name AS campaign,

    device.category AS device_category,
    geo.country AS country,
    geo.region AS region

  FROM `sino-yoga-494202.analytics_527584579.events_*`
)

SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  CONCAT(user_pseudo_id, '-', CAST(ga_session_id AS STRING)) AS session_key,

  COALESCE(source, '(direct)') AS source,
  COALESCE(medium, '(none)') AS medium,
  COALESCE(campaign, '(not set)') AS campaign,

  ANY_VALUE(page_location) AS landing_page,
  ANY_VALUE(device_category) AS device_category,
  ANY_VALUE(country) AS country,
  ANY_VALUE(region) AS region,

  COUNTIF(event_name = 'page_view') AS page_views,
  SUM(COALESCE(engagement_time_msec, 0)) / 1000 AS engagement_seconds,

  MAX(CASE 
    WHEN event_name IN ('sign_up', 'form_submit', 'purchase') THEN 1 
    ELSE 0 
  END) AS converted,

  MAX(CASE 
    WHEN COALESCE(engagement_time_msec, 0) > 0 THEN 1 
    ELSE 0 
  END) AS engaged_session

FROM base_events
WHERE ga_session_id IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  session_key,
  source,
  medium,
  campaign;

View 2: mart_ga4_daily_channel_kpis_v1

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW `sino-yoga-494202.analytics_527584579.mart_ga4_daily_channel_kpis_v1` AS

-- Daily channel-level KPI table for Looker Studio
-- Aggregates session-level mart into reporting-ready KPIs

SELECT
  event_date,
  source,
  medium,
  campaign,
  device_category,
  country,

  COUNT(DISTINCT session_key) AS sessions,
  COUNT(DISTINCT user_pseudo_id) AS users,
  SUM(page_views) AS page_views,
  SUM(engagement_seconds) AS engagement_seconds,
  SUM(converted) AS conversions,
  SUM(engaged_session) AS engaged_sessions,

  SAFE_DIVIDE(SUM(engaged_session), COUNT(DISTINCT session_key)) AS engagement_rate,
  SAFE_DIVIDE(SUM(converted), COUNT(DISTINCT session_key)) AS conversion_rate,
  SAFE_DIVIDE(SUM(engagement_seconds), COUNT(DISTINCT session_key)) AS avg_engagement_seconds_per_session

FROM `sino-yoga-494202.analytics_527584579.mart_ga4_session_summary_v1`

GROUP BY
  event_date,
  source,
  medium,
  campaign,
  device_category,
  country;

Key Insights from Studio Dashboard
The Studio dashboard provides a snapshot of Sino Yoga’s website performance and shows that the site is still in an early growth stage with limited engagement.
Overall performance
Early Growth, Low Engagement

148 users = small but growing awareness
20 sec avg session + 89% bounce rate

Users leave quickly = weak first impression & unclear value


Traffic & Acquisition Issues
Direct traffic dominates

Likely from YouTube/social, not scalable Low SEO & paid traffic
Poor discoverability = limited growth potential

User Behavior Gaps

Mostly new users (low retention)
Low interaction + no page exploration

Weak engagement & unclear user journey
Content & Funnel Problem

/en-us = main entry page (almost all traffic)
Other pages barely visited

No content flow → no conversion funnel

title: Final Client Package
text:
Attribution Modeling Approach

Moves beyond descriptive insights to understand how channels contribute across the user journey
Uses engaged sessions as a proxy for conversion
(due to lack of purchase/membership tracking in GA4)
Recognizes that user journeys are multi-touch, not single-channel
Uses a simplified path-based dataset to approximate real user journeys
Evaluates channel contribution across awareness, consideration, and engagement

title: Final Client Package
text:
Attribution Models Used
Four attribution approaches are applied to understand channel contribution across the funnel:

First-Touch -> Awareness (first interaction)
Last-Touch -> Conversion (final interaction)
Linear -> Equal credit across journey
Markov Chain -> Most impactful channels

title: Final Client Package
text:
Key Insights from Attribution Analysis

YouTube plays a critical awareness role
Primary entry point for users, supporting initial discovery and brand exposure.
Direct traffic dominates final interactions
Many users return directly, suggesting that users revisit the site after initial exposure rather than converting immediately.
Search and cross-channel interactions support mid-funnel behavior
Channels such as Google Search appear within multi-step journeys, indicating that users seek additional information before engaging further.
User journeys are multi-touch, not single-channel
Engagement is driven by a combination of channels working together rather than a single source.

title: Final Client Package
text:
Business Interpretation
These findings indicate that Sino Yoga’s current marketing funnel is:

Strong in awareness generation
Weak in structured conversion pathways

Evaluating performance using only last-touch attribution would underestimate the value of YouTube and other top-of-funnel channels. A multi-touch perspective shows that different platforms contribute at different stages of the user journey.
To improve performance, Sino Yoga should focus on:

Strengthening connections between YouTube and the website
Introducing clearer calls-to-action (CTAs)
Developing intermediate “bridge content” to guide users toward conversion

title: Final Client Package
text:
Model Limitations

Path data is simulated and does not represent real user-level journeys
Cross-platform tracking is limited (e.g., YouTube to website attribution)

Despite these limitations, the analysis provides directional insights into how channels contribute to user engagement and highlights opportunities to improve the conversion funnel.

title: Final Client Package
text:
Recommendations
Six data-driven recommendations grounded in dashboard findings:

Conversion Event Tracking — implement signup, membership, and engagement events in GA4 (currently 0 tracked)
Bounce Rate Reduction — landing page optimization to address 89% bounce rate and 20-second sessions
UTM Tracking Activation — tag external links to surface YouTube, WeChat, and social referrals
Returning Visitor Pathways — email capture, content calendar, and retargeting for retention
Multi-Page Content Journey — strengthen internal links, embed videos, expand beyond /en-us
Audience Localization — prioritize the existing Singapore audience (70%) alongside U.S. growth

title: Final Client Package
text:
Limitations
Data Pipeline

4-week reporting window — directional findings, not established trends
GA4 daily export still maturing
Mart-to-dashboard integration in progress

Methodology

Engagement used as proxy for conversion
Single-touchpoint dominance limits multi-touch attribution
Sample size of 148 users

Scope

Website-only analysis
No revenue or transactional data available

title: Final Client Package
text:
Next Steps
Measurement Foundation (Immediate)

Add UTM-tagged website link to YouTube channel “About” page and top-performing video descriptions
Apply UTM tagging template to every new video at upload going forward
Reconnect the dashboard to the BigQuery mart for ongoing reporting

Build Out the Pipeline (1–3 Months)

Allow daily GA4 export tables to mature in BigQuery
Begin tracking cross-channel referral patterns through UTM data
Identify which YouTube content actually drives website traffic

Strategic Iteration (3+ Months)

Re-run attribution analysis with real multi-touch user paths
Evaluate which YouTube content categories drive the strongest website engagement
Use these insights to inform content strategy and any future website investments

title: Final Client Package
section: R Code Example Using Sino Yoga Journey Data
text:
R Code Example Using Sino Yoga Journey Data

library(tidyverse)
library(ChannelAttribution)
library(gt)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)

# Example Sino Yoga attribution data
sino_paths <- tibble(
  user_id = c("U001", "U002", "U003", "U004", "U005", "U006", "U007", "U008"),
  path = c(
    "YouTube > Website > Direct",
    "Google Search > Website",
    "Facebook > YouTube > Website",
    "YouTube > Google Search > Website",
    "Direct > Website",
    "WeChat > YouTube > Website",
    "YouTube > Website",
    "Google Search > YouTube > Website > Direct"
  ),
  conversion = c(1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1)
)

# Prepare data for ChannelAttribution package
channel_stack <- sino_paths |>
  group_by(path) |>
  summarise(
    conversion = sum(conversion),
    non_conversion = n() - sum(conversion),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

channel_stack

# A tibble: 8 × 3
  path                                       conversion non_conversion
  <chr>                                           <dbl>          <dbl>
1 Direct > Website                                    0              1
2 Facebook > YouTube > Website                        1              0
3 Google Search > Website                             0              1
4 Google Search > YouTube > Website > Direct          1              0
5 WeChat > YouTube > Website                          1              0
6 YouTube > Google Search > Website                   1              0
7 YouTube > Website                                   1              0
8 YouTube > Website > Direct                          1              0

title: Final Client Package
text:
First Touch Attribution

first_touch <- sino_paths |>
  filter(conversion == 1) |>
  mutate(first_channel = str_trim(str_split_fixed(path, ">", 10)[,1])) |>
  count(first_channel, name = "conversions") |>
  mutate(
    attribution_model = "First Touch",
    credit_share = conversions / sum(conversions)
  )

first_touch |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_percent(columns = credit_share, decimals = 1) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "First-Touch Attribution",
    subtitle = "Which channels introduce converting users?"
  )

title: Final Client Package

text:
Markov Chain Attribution

markov_model_result <- markov_model(
  Data = channel_stack,
  var_path = "path",
  var_conv = "conversion",
  var_null = "non_conversion",
  out_more = TRUE
)


Number of simulations: 100000 - Convergence reached: 0.88% < 5.00%

Percentage of simulated paths that successfully end before maximum number of steps (5) is reached: 97.63%

[1] "*** Install ChannelAttribution Pro for free running install_pro(). Visit https://channelattribution.io for more info. Set flg_pro=FALSE to hide this message."

markov_results <- markov_model_result$result |>
  as_tibble() |>
  mutate(
    credit_share = total_conversions / sum(total_conversions)
  )

markov_results |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = total_conversions, decimals = 2) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = credit_share, decimals = 1) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Markov Chain Attribution",
    subtitle = "Channel contribution based on removal effect"
  )

title: Final Client Package
text:
Interpretation
This attribution analysis in theory will helps Sino Yoga understand which channels support the full customer journey. YouTube may not always be the final conversion channel, but it likely plays a major role in awareness, education, and trust-building. The website may close the conversion, while Google Search or Direct traffic may reflect users returning after earlier exposure. Therefore, Sino Yoga should not judge performance only by last-touch conversions. Instead, it should use multi-touch attribution to understand how YouTube, search, website, and social platforms work together to move users toward membership.

text: Template

title: Student Performance Prediction Using Random Forest Modeling
text:
Overview
This project focused on analyzing factors that influence academic student performance using machine learning techniques. Using a Kaggle Student Performance dataset containing 10,000 student records, the goal was to predict student performance based on study habits, previous scores, extracurricular participation, sleep patterns, and practice exam completion. The project emphasized data cleaning, predictive modeling, and evaluating the effectiveness of machine learning algorithms in educational analytics.




Outcomes

The Random Forest model demonstrated exceptionally strong predictive accuracy:
R²: 0.986 (explained 98.6% of performance variation)
RMSE: 2.40
MAE: 1.89




Key Skills Gained
The dataset was cleaned and transformed by standardizing variable names, converting categorical variables into numeric dummy variables, and selecting relevant predictor variables. Data preprocessing included normalization and removal of missing values using recipe workflows in R.
The project used a Random Forest regression model to predict the dependent variable, PerformanceIndex. The dataset was split into training (80%) and testing (20%) sets to evaluate model performance. The workflow included:

Data cleaning and feature engineering
Exploratory data validation
Model training using Random Forest regression
Performance evaluation using RMSE, R², and MAE metrics




The results showed that factors such as study hours, previous academic performance, sleep habits, extracurricular involvement, and practice papers were highly predictive of student success. The model effectively minimized prediction error and demonstrated the potential for machine learning applications in educational performance analysis and early intervention strategies.

title: Customer Retention Analysis Project
text:
Overview
This project focused on designing and executing a customer satisfaction and brand loyalty survey for a fictional regional coffee chain, Bean There Done That, to analyze declining repeat purchase behavior. The research aimed to identify how customer satisfaction, product quality, and price perceptions influence customer retention and revisit intentions. The project combined survey research methodology with statistical analysis to generate actionable managerial insights for improving customer loyalty and repeat visits.




Learning Outcomes

Developed experience designing research-based surveys using marketing constructs and academic literature
Strengthened understanding of customer satisfaction, brand loyalty, and consumer behavior analysis
Learned how to apply quantitative research methods to real-world business problems
Gained experience conducting regression analysis and Chi-Square testing using SPSS
Improved ability to interpret statistical findings and translate them into business recommendations
Enhanced understanding of how data-driven insights support customer retention strategies and decision-making




Key Skills Gained

Statistical Analysis: Conducted multiple regression analysis and Chi-Square testing to evaluate customer behavior relationships.
Consumer Behavior Analysis: Examined how satisfaction, presentation quality, and emotional experiences influence revisit intentions.
Data Preparation: Transformed and prepared survey data for SPSS analysis, including converting categorical variables into numerical scales.
Managerial Insight Development: Created actionable recommendations focused on customer satisfaction, loyalty programs, and service improvements.
Research Communication: Synthesized analytical findings into professional business research reports and presentations.
Marketing Analytics: Applied quantitative methods to evaluate customer retention and loyalty drivers.
Strategic Problem Solving: Used research findings to recommend strategies for improving customer experience and repeat purchase behavior.





Methodology & Visualization
The project used a structured survey research methodology supported by academic journal references and conceptual frameworks related to customer satisfaction, product quality, price consciousness, and brand loyalty. Survey responses from 200 generated participants were analyzed using SPSS through descriptive statistics, multiple regression analysis, ANOVA testing, cross-tabulation, and Chi-Square testing.
Visual elements included conceptual framework diagrams, descriptive statistics tables, coefficient tables, ANOVA outputs, and crosstab visualizations to communicate statistical relationships and analytical findings effectively

title: Final Project
text:
Cleaning Student Performance Data
We cleaned the data by importing library models tidyverse, tidymodels, rio, and janitor. We set the seed to set.seed(123) to keep the same results.
Using the the function import() from the rio libary we imported the downloaded data that we renamed to Student_Performance.csv into a new dataset called StudentData. Doing this we cleaned up the names using upper camel with all the variables. We then mutated the variable ExtracurricularActivities to Extracurriculars to shorten the name. We also made it into a categorical variable using 1 for Yes and 0 for No. We also mutated the variable SampleQuestionPapersPracticed into the name SamplePapers to be easier to code. We then selected the new variables HoursStudied, , PreviousScores, Extracurriculars, SleepHours, SamplePapers, PerformanceIndex. To make sure this was all done we showed the top six result of the dataset StudentData. Checking the summary() function we could see if there was any missing NA values and there were not. We then checked the str() function to make sure the Extracurriculars was changed to a integer instead of a character value. Finally we then used the write() funciton to save the new tidy data as “Student_Performance_Clean.csv” as a new downloadable excel file.

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(rio)
library(janitor)
#install.packages("ranger")
set.seed(123) #make sure we get the same result

# Import and clean data
StudentData <- import("Student_Performance.csv") |> #importing downloaded data 
  clean_names("upper_camel") |>  # Capitalize variable names
  mutate(Extracurriculars = if_else(ExtracurricularActivities == "Yes", 1L, 0L), #1L and )L ensures integer data types
    SamplePapers = SampleQuestionPapersPracticed
  ) |> #mutate chr to int as dummy var and clean names
  select(HoursStudied, PreviousScores, Extracurriculars, SleepHours, SamplePapers, PerformanceIndex) #select correct variables
  head(StudentData) #show the top 6 observations of the dataset to preview

  write.csv(StudentData, "Student_Performance_Clean.csv", row.names = FALSE)#creates new csv with tidy data

title: Final Project
text:
Variables

Predictor Variables (Independent Variables): HoursStudied, PrevoiusScores, Extracurriculars, SleepHours, SamplePapers
Outcome Variable(Dependent Variable): Performance Index

title: Final Project
text:
Machine Learning Algorithm
The machine learning algorithm we chose was Random Forest Model.

# Split data into training and testing sets
splitStudentData <- initial_split(StudentData, prop = 0.8, strata = PerformanceIndex)
DataTrain <- training(splitStudentData)
DataTest <- testing(splitStudentData)

# Create a recipe
RecipeStudent <- recipe(PerformanceIndex ~ HoursStudied + PreviousScores + Extracurriculars + SleepHours + SamplePapers, data = DataTrain) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_naomit()

# Specify Random Forest model
ModelStudent <- rand_forest(mtry = 2, trees = 500, min_n = 5) |>
  set_engine("ranger") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# Create a workflow
WFStudent <- workflow() |>
  add_model(ModelStudent) %>%
  add_recipe(RecipeStudent)

# Fit the model
FitStudent <- fit(WFStudent, data = DataTrain)

# Predict on test set
PredictStudent <- predict(FitStudent, DataTest) |>
  bind_cols(DataTest |> select(PerformanceIndex))

# Evaluate
metrics <- PredictStudent |>
  metrics(truth = PerformanceIndex, estimate = .pred)

print(metrics)

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  .metric .estimator .estimate
  <chr>   <chr>          <dbl>
1 rmse    standard       2.40 
2 rsq     standard       0.986
3 mae     standard       1.89

title: Final Project
text:
RMSE
The Random Forest model demonstrated strong predictive accuracy, as reflected in its Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 2.39. This means that on average, the model’s predicted student scores deviate by only 2.39 points from the actual scores. A low RMSE indicates that the model consistently produces close estimates and avoids large prediction errors. These results suggest that the model is highly reliable in estimating student performance and can be trusted to make accurate predictions across a range of academic profiles.

title: Final Project
text:
RSQ
The Random Forest model demonstrated exceptional predictive performance on the test dataset. The model achieved an R² value of 0.986, indicating that it explains approximately 98.6% of the variation in student performance based on study hours, previous scores, extracurricular participation, sleep hours, and practice papers.

title: Final Project
text:
MAE
The Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 1.89. These results suggest that the model provides highly accurate predictions and effectively captures the key factors influencing student performance.

title: Final Project
text:
Conclusion
Our analysis of the Student Performance dataset reveals that academic performance can be predicted with high accuracy using factors such as hours studied, previous test scores, participation in extracurricular activities, average sleep hours, and the number of practice papers completed. The Random Forest model’s outstanding results explaining nearly all variation in performance and maintaining prediction errors within roughly 2 points highlight the strong relationship between these predictors and overall student success. These findings suggest that a balanced approach combining consistent study habits, adequate rest, and targeted practice can significantly enhance academic outcomes. This model could be a valuable tool for educators and policymakers to identify at-risk students and implement strategies to improve performance.

title: Sino Yoga Data Studio Project
text:
Sino Yoga Data Studio Project

Overview
This project focused on analyzing Sino Yoga’s digital performance through an interactive analytics dashboard created in Google Data Studio (Looker Studio). The dashboard integrated website and YouTube performance metrics to evaluate audience engagement, search visibility, traffic behavior, and cross-platform growth opportunities. The primary objective was to assess how YouTube content contributed to website traffic, brand awareness, and customer engagement across digital channels
The project emphasized the importance of multi-channel integration and data-driven marketing strategies to support Sino Yoga’s long-term brand growth and monetization goals. Metrics such as website sessions, engagement trends, bounce rates, referral traffic, and audience interactions were analyzed to identify actionable insights for improving digital performance.




Learning Outcomes

Developed experience creating and interpreting interactive marketing dashboards using Google Data Studio (Looker Studio)
Strengthened understanding of website analytics, YouTube performance tracking, and SEO visibility analysis
Learned how to connect and visualize multi-channel marketing data to support strategic decision-making
Improved ability to analyze customer engagement and traffic behavior across digital platforms
Applied marketing analytics concepts to evaluate awareness-stage customer journey performance
Gained insight into how digital dashboards support reporting, optimization, and performance monitoring in marketing environments




Key Skills Gained

Data Visualization: Created dashboards and visual reports to communicate marketing insights clearly and effectively using Google Data Studio.
Marketing Analytics: Analyzed website traffic, YouTube engagement, and referral data to evaluate campaign and channel performance.
SEO & Channel Visibility Analysis: Assessed search performance and discoverability opportunities for Sino Yoga’s website and YouTube content.
Performance Tracking: Monitored KPIs including website sessions, engagement rates, referral traffic, and audience behavior.
Cross-Platform Integration: Evaluated how YouTube traffic contributed to website engagement and brand growth across channels.
Strategic Reporting: Synthesized analytical findings into actionable recommendations for improving digital growth and audience engagement.
Digital Marketing Strategy: Applied data-driven insights to support brand awareness and monetization strategies for a wellness brand.

title: Marketing Analytics

title: Marketing Analytics
text:
Overview
This project focused on analyzing factors that influence academic student performance using machine learning techniques. Using a Kaggle Student Performance dataset containing 10,000 student records, the goal was to predict student performance based on study habits, previous scores, extracurricular participation, sleep patterns, and practice exam completion. The project emphasized data cleaning, predictive modeling, and evaluating the effectiveness of machine learning algorithms in educational analytics.

Tools Used

R Programming Language



Methodology

Data cleaning and feature engineering
Exploratory data validation
Model training using Random Forest regression
Performance evaluation using RMSE, R², and MAE metrics



Visualizations
The project included dataset summaries, variable structure analysis, and model evaluation metrics to interpret predictive performance. Statistical summaries and performance metrics were used to visualize the relationships between student behaviors and academic outcomes. The workflow also emphasized reproducibility and clean data practices through structured preprocessing and workflow pipelines in R.

title: Marketing Analytics
text:
Overview
This project focused on designing and executing a customer satisfaction and brand loyalty survey for a fictional regional coffee chain, Bean There Done That, to analyze declining repeat purchase behavior. The research aimed to identify how customer satisfaction, product quality, and price perceptions influence customer retention and revisit intentions. The project combined survey research methodology with statistical analysis to generate actionable managerial insights for improving customer loyalty and repeat visits.

Learning Outcomes

Developed experience designing research-based surveys using marketing constructs and academic literature



Strengthened understanding of customer satisfaction, brand loyalty, and consumer behavior analysis



Learned how to apply quantitative research methods to real-world business problems



Gained experience conducting regression analysis and Chi-Square testing using SPSS



Improved ability to interpret statistical findings and translate them into business recommendations



Enhanced understanding of how data-driven insights support customer retention strategies and decision-making



Methodology

The project used a structured survey research methodology supported by academic journal references and conceptual frameworks related to customer satisfaction,product quality, price consciousness, and brand loyalty.
Survey responses from 200 generated participants were analyzed using SPSS through descriptive statistics, multiple regression analysis, ANOVA testing, cross-tabulation, and Chi-Square testing.

title: Marketing Analytics
text:
Overview

The dashboard integrated website and YouTube performance metrics to evaluate audience engagement, search visibility, traffic behavior, and cross-platform growth opportunities. The primary objective was to assess how YouTube content contributed to website traffic, brand awareness, and customer engagement across digital channels
The project emphasized the importance of multi-channel integration and data-driven marketing strategies to support Sino Yoga’s long-term brand growth and monetization goals. title: Marketing Analytics
text:
Overview
Sino Yoga is a yoga and wellness brand that primarily broadcasts content through YouTube. Founded by Yang Qi, who has over 18 years of yoga expertise, the brand aims to expand its awareness and increase monetization opportunities through consistent digital growth and regular video uploads.

title: Marketing Analytics
text:
Data
Using YouTube Studio Data as csv files while cleaning the data and creating a Tableau interactive link to show SEO Performance and overall channel visibility.
Data CSV Links:

Tableau SEO Dashboard
As seen on this dashboard the audience is primarily 45 years and over females mainly from Taiwan dn the US. This strongly aligns with out clients goal of expanding to those target markets. That viewers are primarily using mobile devices to view the videos. This emphasizes the need to focus on mobile optimized content for all the videos. 
Regarding traffic to the YouTube channel, Sino Yoga relies largely on external traffic sources such as WeChat and Naver while YouTube & Google Search contribute to low traffic, indicating weak organic search visibility.
Inside of the YouTube platform traffic from vidoes shows CTR being 9-10% from Youtube search however the amount of times the video is shown or impressions are low compared to browse features traffic source. This suggest that while content performs well when discovered it lacks sufficient visibility in search results. 
Tableau Interactive Link



Time Series Plot
The time series plot shows the daily data of videos published regarding their impressions and over the channel’s lifetime. Initially starting with high ctr and high impressions but stabilizing between 4-7% indicate incosistent visibility. While the ability to create effective thumbnails and title, there is a lack of consistency and uniformity in optimization. Impressions decreasing also show that there is a low visibility throughout the entire video uploads. 
Tableu Link to Interactive



Keyword Research
The keyword analysis shows that while high-volume terms like yoga, health, and meditation drive the most search demand, they are highly competitive and difficult to rank for. In contrast, lower-competition keywords such as stretching, meridian, qi, and flexibility still offer meaningful search volume with a higher chance of ranking. This highlights a clear opportunity for Sino Yoga to shift toward niche, lower-difficulty keywords, enabling faster visibility in search and building stronger SEO performance over time. 
Tableu Interactive Link
